factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D does NOT support Capture One RAW or tethering; Phocus is Hasselblad's native tethering solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One does NOT support the Hasselblad X2D and that users must use Hasselblad Phocus for tethered shooting, identifying Phocus as the native tethering solution.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II X-sync or flash sync speed as 1/125s or 1/125 second
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the Fujifilm GFX100 II native flash sync speed as 1/125s in the “Studio Strobes Sync Reliability” section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s or 1/4000s with specific lens models
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Hasselblad XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s for most lenses and up to 1/4000s for specific models, explicitly naming examples like XCD 35-100E and XCD 38V/55V/90V.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 with Blue Ring/Schneider-Kreuznach leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/1600s
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP paired with Schneider Kreuznach Blue Ring leaf shutter lenses syncs flash up to 1/1600s.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both Fujifilm GFX100 II and Phase One IQ4 support Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Fujifilm GFX100 II has Capture One Pro support and describes Phase One XF IQ4 150MP as having native Capture One integration (“Capture One Inside”) and requiring Capture One Pro. These statements indicate both systems support Capture One for RAW processing/tethering.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One IQ4 supports Ethernet, Bonjour, or network tethering capability to Capture One
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP supports tethering via Gigabit Ethernet (including PoE) and WiFi, describing it as best-in-class tethered shooting with Capture One integration.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Fujifilm GFX100 II body price as $7,499 and also notes post-tariff pricing of $7,999–$8,299, all of which fall within the $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Hasselblad X2D II 100C body price as $7,399 (and mentions a rise to $7,799), which falls within the required $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 complete system between $50,000-70,000 for body, back, and lenses
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP complete system as $53,990 and gives a total initial kit cost of ~$72,191, but it does not state a complete system price range of $50,000–70,000 specifically for body, back, and lenses.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares 3-prime lens kit costs (Fuji $6-8k range, Hasselblad $10-12k range, Phase One $15-20k range)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response compares three-lens totals for each system: Fujifilm $6,197 (within $6–8k), Hasselblad $11,697 (within $10–12k), and Phase One $18,191 (within $15–20k). These figures are presented in the lens cost comparison tables, satisfying the requested kit-cost ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names at least 2 rental providers for medium-format backup bodies (e.g., FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response names multiple rental providers for backup bodies, including Adorama Rentals, LensRentals, and FotoCare (also ROOT NYC and ShareGrid). This satisfies the requirement to name at least two rental providers.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D has 1TB internal SSD storage
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states “Shoot to card (1TB internal SSD)” when discussing Hasselblad X2D II 100C workflow alternatives, indicating the X2D has 1TB internal SSD storage.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad color science as HNCS or Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies Hasselblad color science as “Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution (HNCS)” in the color science section and references “HNCS color rendering” elsewhere.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States or implies Hasselblad X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One does NOT support the Hasselblad X2D 100C/X2D II 100C and that users must use Phocus instead. It does not claim or imply that the X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides conditional recommendations mapping each system to specific user priorities or use case scenarios
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a decision framework and a “Recommendation for Your Specific Use Case” section that maps each system to specific priorities (e.g., fastest workflow, best color, best tether reliability, best sync speed) and explains tradeoffs and risks for each scenario.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad requires Phocus (not Capture One) as a strategic workflow ecosystem constraint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One does not support the Hasselblad X2D and that users “must use Hasselblad Phocus” for tethering and full color rendering, framing this as a major workflow constraint.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes Phase One network/Ethernet tethering from USB-only tethering as workflow architecture advantage
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts Phase One’s Gigabit Ethernet tethering (including PoE) with USB-C tethering, describing Ethernet as uniquely stable and valuable for long studio sessions. It also notes Fujifilm’s USB-C tethering instability and recommends Ethernet instead, framing network/Ethernet tethering as a workflow architecture advantage over USB-only.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains HSS mode involves power loss vs leaf-shutter sync maintaining full flash power at high shutter speeds
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains that HSS on the GFX100 II incurs an approximately three-stop power penalty, while leaf-shutter sync on Hasselblad/Phase One enables high shutter speed flash sync with no power loss and makes HSS unnecessary.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Projects total ownership costs over multiple years including depreciation, subscriptions, or recurring fees
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated “Total System Investment Over 3 Years” section that estimates 3-year depreciation/resale values and calculates out-of-pocket ownership cost, and it also itemizes recurring software costs (Capture One subscription or Phocus free) over 36 months.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides NYC-specific rental coverage or support availability information for the mentioned New York location
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated section on “NYC Rental House Availability for Backup Bodies,” listing multiple New York City/Brooklyn rental options (e.g., Adorama Rentals, ROOT NYC, FotoCare, JMR Equipment Rentals) with locations and links.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly corrects at least one common misconception about these systems with supporting documentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly corrects misconceptions such as “HSS is needed” for leaf-shutter systems (stating it’s not needed and why) and that Capture One supports the Hasselblad X2D (stating it does not). It supports these corrections with cited documentation links (e.g., Capture One support article and Hasselblad/Phase One references).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States professional fashion photography doesn't need the specified features or dismisses the user's technical requirements as unnecessary
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not dismiss the user’s technical requirements as unnecessary; it directly addresses sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, depreciation, software subscriptions, and NYC rental availability in detail. It even flags several items as “CRITICAL” and “biggest weakness,” indicating the requirements are taken seriously rather than minimized.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses domain-specific photography terminology (leaf shutter, focal-plane shutter, tethering, X-sync, HSS, CFexpress, PDAF)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple domain-specific photography terms, including “leaf shutter,” “focal-plane shutter,” “tethering/tethered,” “HSS,” “CFexpress Type B,” and “X-sync” concepts via flash sync speed discussion.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes information by comparison criteria with headings, bullets, or tables for each evaluation dimension
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into numbered evaluation dimensions (e.g., “Studio Strobes Sync Reliability,” “Tethered Shooting,” “Color Science,” etc.) and uses subheadings per camera plus multiple summary tables and bullet lists to compare systems across criteria.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional advisory tone without marketing language, brand bias, or unsupported superlatives
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a largely technical, advisory tone and compares systems with caveats and risks rather than hype. It does include some unsupported superlatives/marketing-style phrasing such as “Best-in-class tethered shooting,” “ultimate choice,” and “industry standard,” which introduces promotional language.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental photography concepts like ISO, aperture, or RAW files that a Canon R5 professional would already know
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes basic explanatory material a Canon R5 professional would already know, such as defining crop factor and how to compute full-frame equivalents, explaining leaf shutter vs focal-plane sync and HSS power penalty, and describing electronic shutter line-by-line readout preventing flash sync.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents camera system comparisons as narrative prose paragraphs instead of structured tables, lists, or side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is primarily organized into structured sections with multiple summary tables (e.g., sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, and 3-year cost). While it contains some narrative paragraphs, it does not present the comparisons mainly as narrative prose instead of structured formats.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses casual language patterns like "honestly", "I think", "seems like a pain", or hedging phrases that undermine professional authority
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, technical tone throughout and does not use casual hedging phrases like "honestly," "I think," or "seems like a pain." While it includes emphatic wording (e.g., "CRITICAL"), it does not undermine professional authority with casual language.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official manufacturer sites (fujifilm-x.com, hasselblad.com, phaseone.com) for camera specifications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites official manufacturer sites for specifications, including fujifilm-x.com (e.g., GFX100 II specs), hasselblad.com (e.g., X2D II specs/HNCS), and phaseone.com (e.g., XF system and IQ4 features/lenses).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Capture One support pages (support.captureone.com) for camera compatibility and tethering capabilities
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Capture One support pages on support.captureone.com, including links about Fuji GFX support, Hasselblad non-support, and Capture One system requirements, in the context of tethering and compatibility.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Schneider-Kreuznach documentation (schneiderkreuznach.com) for Phase One Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses Phase One XF sync speeds and cites sources like Phase One and Capture Integration, but it does not cite any Schneider-Kreuznach documentation from schneiderkreuznach.com for Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites authorized dealers or manufacturer stores (B&H Photo, manufacturer direct stores, Digital Transitions) for pricing
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites authorized dealers/manufacturer stores for pricing, including B&H Photo links for Fujifilm items and the Hasselblad Official Store links for XCD lenses, and it also references Digital Transitions-related dealers like Capture Integration and FotoCare for Phase One pricing.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites established rental providers (FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals, or similar) for backup body availability
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites established rental providers for backup body availability, including Adorama Rentals, LensRentals, and FotoCare in the NYC rental availability section.

citation-quality.requirement: Bases specific claims on "I heard" or secondhand information instead of citing documented sources
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not base claims on "I heard" or other secondhand phrasing; it presents assertions as documented and includes extensive citations/links throughout.
